exemplar.title: Is it simpler to recall a list of words or a list of images?

detailedExemplarData.title: Is it simpler to recall a list of words or a list of images?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear articulation of investigation aim and its educational relevance
Thorough description of the Working Memory Model with explicit links to experimental conditions
Independent and dependent variables are correctly stated and fully operationalized
weaknesses: Omission of specific capacity limits for each subsystem of the model
Lack of citation for certain model claims (e.g., visuospatial sketchpad capacity)
Occasional vague phrasing (e.g., 'primary subjects') that could be clarified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction consistently meets the band-5 descriptors across all strands: the aim is stated and its relevance explained, the theoretical model is described with clear links to the investigation, and the hypothesis accurately operationalizes IV and DV. Minor omissions (capacity figures, citation accuracy) prevent a top-band 6 but justify a strong 5.

strengths: Independent-samples design is not only described but justified in terms of reducing demand characteristics
Sampling technique and participant demographics are explained along with representativeness limitations
Controlled variables and material choices are clearly justified to ensure task consistency
weaknesses: Ethical referencing could cite specific guidelines (e.g., APA, BPS)
Details on the technical reliability of the timing mechanism (Canva) are not provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of exploration are fully explained rather than merely described. The student justifies design, sampling and materials with clear rationale, placing performance securely at the top of band 3â4 (4/4). Minor expansions (ethical guidelines, timing reliability) would enhance completeness but do not detract from full-marks for this criterion.

strengths: Inferential conclusion correctly reports non-significance and acceptance of the null hypothesis
Interpretation of descriptive statistics links medians and outliers to the hypothesis
weaknesses: Inconsistent choice of an independent-samples t-test when medians suggest a non-parametric test would be more appropriate
Graphical presentations contain labelling and scale errors (unlabeled axes, inconsistent plotting of medians)
Misalignment of p-value threshold (p<.01 used instead of conventional p<.05)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student applies both descriptive and inferential statistics and offers a correct interpretation of findings, methodological errors (test choice, variance notation), graphical mislabelling and unconventional significance thresholds undermine precision. These shortcomings align with band 1â2 for some strands and band 3â4 for others; overall a 3/6 reflects moderate performance with key errors.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to the Working Memory Model and relevant previous research
Strengths and limitations are explicitly linked to specific aspects of the study and well explained
Proposed modifications directly address identified limitations and are well justified
weaknesses: Discussion could be enriched by integrating alternative theories (e.g., dual-coding model)
Depth of evaluation is solid but could be expanded with additional critical perspectives or further modifications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates genuine discussion of findings in light of theory, clear articulation of strengths/limitations, and justified methodological improvements. Some depth is missing (broader theoretical integration and further modifications), positioning the work in band 3â4. A score of 4/6 fairly reflects strong but not exhaustive evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: A boxplot and a bar graph with error bars are provided and both relate directly to the hypothesis.  Nonetheless, axes on the bar graph are only partially labelled and the bar chart uses the median yet plots interval marks inconsistently.  These presentation flaws fit the âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errorsâ description in band 3-4, meriting a mid-band 3.

The student states statistical findings (medians, p-value) and explicitly interprets themâacceptance of Hâ, explanation of what this means for recallâand links this back to the experimental data and hypothesis.  Despite the small statistical mismatch noted earlier, the interpretation itself is accurate and firmly connected to both data sets and hypothesis, satisfying band 5-6.  Minor wording weaknesses place it at 5 rather than 6.

Both IV and DV are correctly identified and fully operationalised: IV = presentation of 20 images vs 20 verbal labels at 4-second intervals; DV = number of items written down immediately after exposure.  The null and research hypotheses are correctly phrased in terms of medians and the specific participant group.  This satisfies the top-band descriptor; wording slips ("retain" vs "recall") are minor, so 5/6 is appropriate.

The student describes convenience sampling and provides age, gender and language background, then explains why this method was chosen (accessibility, familiarity with IB) and acknowledges representativeness limitations.  That explicit rationale moves the work from description to explanation, matching band 3-4.  The explanation is adequate rather than sophisticated, so 4/4.

The Working Memory Model is described in reasonable detail (central executive, phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad) and the student explicitly connects each subsystem to the two experimental conditions, explaining why images should rely on the visuospatial sketchpad and words on the phonological loop.  This meets the requirement that the theory is described and the link to the investigation is explained.  Some technical depth is missing (e.g., capacity limits quoted without citation accuracy), so a cautious 5 in band 5-6 is most defensible.

The discussion reconnects the non-significant findings to the Working Memory Model and to previous research (Hasher et al.), offering a reason (lists were short/simple) why no difference emerged.  That constitutes a genuine discussion rather than mere description, fulfilling the band 5-6 descriptor.  Depth is moderate, so a 5 is appropriate.

The student clearly states the aim â to find out whether lists of images or lists of words are easier to recall â and explicitly explains why this matters for educational practice and memory-improvement strategies.  The explanation links the investigation to real-world learning contexts rather than merely noting that it is ârelevant,â so the response fits the descriptor for a full explanation (band 5â6).  Minor brevity in the discussion of wider applications prevents a secure 6, but the work is comfortably above the 3-4 level.

Strengths and limitations for design, sample and procedure are explicitly linked to the investigation and each is explained (e.g., independent design avoids order effects but individual memory differences uncontrolled).  The content goes beyond simple listing, meeting band 5-6.  Explanations are clear but not exhaustive, justifying 5.

Key controls (fixed 4-second exposure, identical word lists, prohibition of communication) and choice of Canva and Croatian translations are explained in terms of keeping exposure equal and task difficulty constant.  This goes beyond mere listing, fulfilling the âexplainedâ criterion in band 3-4.  Detail is sound, so the upper mark of the band is appropriate.

The student proposes two concrete modifications (use clearer single-object images; introduce fixed delay before recall to reduce recency) and ties each directly to the corresponding limitation.  Justifications show why the change would improve validity.  This matches the top-band descriptor; while more than two modifications could be offered, the link and justification are sufficient for a 5.

The independent-samples design is not only described but also justified (reduces demand characteristics compared with repeated measures and keeps task difficulty constant).  That justification constitutes an explanation rather than simple listing, which fits band 3-4.  The explanation is concise but clear, warranting the top of that band (4).

Both descriptive (median, SD, boxplots) and inferential (independent t-test) statistics are applied.  Calculations are shown; however, the choice of t-test while emphasising medians, and a small computational slip in pooled variance notation, indicate minor errors.  This matches the descriptor for band 3-4 â appropriate tests but with errors â so 4/6 is justified.

annotations.comment: The investigation aim is stated and its educational relevance is noted; elaborating on specific real-world applications (e.g., study techniques) would deepen the rationale.
Independent variable is clearly operationalized (20 images vs. 20 words at 4-second intervals), demonstrating strong methodological precision.
Both null and research hypotheses correctly identify and operationalize IV and DV, reflecting a clear understanding of experimental variables.
Convenience sampling is described and its limitations acknowledged, demonstrating an understanding of representativeness issues.
Controls (fixed 4-second exposure, identical stimuli, no communication) are well explained and linked to task standardization.
Ethical procedures are well covered; reference specific guidelines (e.g., APA, BPS) to strengthen ethical justification.
The student provides a clear definition of memory with an appropriate citation, grounding the investigationâs foundation.
The Working Memory Model description omits specific capacity limits for each subsystem; including those figures would strengthen technical accuracy.
The statement that the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad are âprimary subjectsâ is vague. Could the student clarify how these subsystems will be empirically compared?
The claim that the visuospatial sketchpad âcan keep more visual informationâ lacks a supporting citation; adding a reference for its capacity would improve credibility.
Please specify the technical reliability of the Canva applicationâs timing mechanism to ensure experimental consistency.
Demographic details (age, gender, language) are clearly reported, enhancing transparency of participant characteristics.
The independent-samples design is not only described but also justified, showing awareness of demand characteristic reduction.
Reference entries vary in format; adopting consistent APA style (e.g., italics, punctuation) will improve scholarly presentation.
Table 1âs caption could more precisely indicate that the reported values are medians for word recall in each condition.
Boxplot (Figure 1) axes and units are unlabeled, which hinders interpretation; please add clear labels and scales.
The interpretation of medians and outliers effectively links descriptive statistics to the hypothesis.
Figure 2 would benefit from explicit axis labels and consistent scale to make error bars and medians immediately clear.
Applying an independent-samples t-test while focusing on medians is inconsistent; consider a MannâWhitney U test for non-parametric analysis.
The inferential conclusion correctly reports non-significance and acceptance of the null hypothesis.
The p-value threshold is set at p < .01 despite conventional use of p < .05; adjusting to standard levels would better align with norms.
Restating the null hypothesis after inferential tests reinforces clarity and ensures the conclusion is unambiguous.
Discussion integrates findings with the Working Memory Model and existing literature, demonstrating solid theoretical evaluation.
Integrating alternative theories such as Paivioâs dual-coding model could enrich the discussion and offer broader perspectives.
Strengths and limitations are appropriately linked to study aspects, illustrating a comprehensive self-evaluation.
Proposed modifications (clearer images, fixed delay) directly address identified limitations and are well justified.
Consider expanding the conclusion to discuss practical implications for teaching and memory-enhancement techniques.
Include a citation for the statistical software or calculator used (e.g., Excel or SocSciStats) to acknowledge tool provenance.
